narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and planned study schedule, focusing on patterns seen in thousands of past students without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the 6th decile average among those who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later undergraduate performance that often correlates with bar outcomes. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.70, well above the 3.24 average for bar passers, which pulled the prediction toward success by highlighting your overall academic capability. Your LSAT score of 34 also helped in that direction, though it fell short of the 37 average for successful students, providing moderate reassurance of your aptitude. Other elements, like your lower-middle family income bracket—less common among bar passers who are more often from middle or upper-middle backgrounds—and your plan to attend part-time, which only 7% of passers did, played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. Compared to typical admitted students, who tend to have higher decile rankings in key years, stronger LSATs, and full-time focus, your profile showed promise in GPA but was held back by that Year 3 dip and other mismatches. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and if possible, reflect on what led to your lower Year 3 ranking to strengthen future applications—perhaps through additional coursework or testimonials. You might also explore programs with part-time tracks that have higher bar passage rates for similar profiles. Many students in your situation improve their odds with targeted prep, like bar-focused tutoring early on. Keep pursuing your goals; reapplying with enhancements could shift the model's view in your favor.
